rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NORMA GALIANO 1987 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Answers To Special Interrogatories (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] The California Supreme Court has stated that even discovery which fails to reveal what the propounding party had hoped for is still "an equally worthy end result. The discovery statutes were intended to curtail surprises, enable each side to learn as much as possible about the strengths and weaknesses of its case, and thereby facilitate realistic settlements and efficient trials. (Williams v. Sup. Ct. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 543, at fn. 3, emphasis in original.) Roberts has also objected to each of the Special Interrogatories at issue on the basis of attorney work product privilege and that they seek expert opinions. Objections must be specific, and the responding/objecting party has the burden of justifying their objections in response to a motion to compel. (Williams v. Sup. Ct. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 541.) That an interrogatory calls for an opinion from the petitioner is not itself a proper objection - "a defendant is entitled to discover by appropriate interrogatories the facts, if any, presently known to the plaintiff upon which he bases the allegations of his complaint and upon which he presently relies to prove his case." (Sheets v. Sup. Ct. (1967) 257 Cal.App.2d 1, 8-9.) Roberts states in the Opposition that the work product objection is merely preventative. Interrogatories may be used to discover the existence of documents. Galiano is correct that the existence of a document containing privileged information is not itself privileged. The responding party must include an adequate description of the responsive document, even if the document itself would be shielded from production. (Hernandez v. Sup. Ct. (2003) 112 Cal.App.4th 285, 293; Best Products, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (2004) 119 Cal.App.4th 1181, 1190.) The court further finds the record demonstrates that Roberts failed to meet and confer regarding her responses to Galiano's Special Interrogatories. (See CCP sections 2030.300(b); 2016.040; 2023.010(i).) Roberts is reminded of her duty engage in reasonable and good faith attempts to resolve informally any dispute concerning discovery. Roberts is ordered to provide the required Code-compliant responses to Specially Prepared Interrogatories No. 1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 10, 12, 19, 21, 38, 39, 40, 46, and 47 within 10 days of the date of service of notice of entry of this order. The court does not find that Roberts acted with substantial justification or that circumstances make imposition of a sanction unjust, thus sanctions are mandatory. (CCP section 2030.300(d).) Roberts is ordered to pay Galiano $60.00, the amount Galiano paid to file this motion. Roberts shall make the payment within 10 days of the date of service of notice of entry of this order. Galiano shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling, prior to the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on August 5, 2021. Remote appearance is available, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925#, participant ID 0#, and passcode 722809#. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 2 of 2]

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE NORMA GALIANO 1987 TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Answers To Special Interrogatories; Points & Authorities In Support; Declaration Of Keth Galiano; Exhibits
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is entered by Judge Joseph M. Quinn. Pending before this court is Respondent Keith Galiano's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Specially Prepared Interrogatories under CCP sections 2030.300 and 2030.220. Petitioner, Leslie Roberts, has filed a First Amended Petition challenging the validity of two amendments to the Norma L. Galiano Revocable Trust on the grounds of undue influence allegedly exercised by Respondent. Respondent propounded the Special Interrogatories at issue in this motion on March 2, 2021. Petitioner served responses electronically on April 1, 2021. After meet and confer efforts, Respondent file this motion on May 13, 2021. Galiano's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Specially Prepared Interrogatories is GRANTED IN FULL. The propounding party has the burden of filing a motion to compel further answers, but the responding party has the burden of justifying any objection or failure to respond in compliance with the Code. The trial court determines what constitutes as adequate response to interrogatories. (See, e.g., Flora Crane Service, Inc. v. Sup. Crt. (1965) 234 Cal.App.2d 767, 782.) Galiano has met the burden of bringing a motion to compel further responses to special interrogatories he feels were inadequately answered. Roberts has not met the corresponding burden to show the responses were adequate under the law, and to justify the objections to the Interrogatories. Roberts' responses to Interrogatory 1, 4, 7, 10, 19, 38, 39, and 40 are inadequate. The five-page narrative response is not fully responsive to each interrogatory separately (CCP section 2030.220(a)). Further, the narrative response includes references to other documents, statements, and events. If a complete response requires reference to some other document, the response should identify the document and summarize its contents so that the answer is internally complete and responsive. (Deyo v. Kilbourne (1978) 84 Cal.App.3d 771, 783-784).) The responses here fail to do that. Unlike a deposition, where the responding party need only rely on their own personal knowledge, in answering interrogatories a party must furnish information available from sources under the party's control. "If the responding party does not have personal knowledge sufficient to respond fully to an interrogatory, that party shall so state, but shall make a reasonable and good faith effort to obtain the information by inquiry to other natural persons or organizations, except where the information is equally available to the propounding party." (CCP section 2030.220(c).) If the responding party has made a reasonable inquiry and still can't obtain the information, they must specify why the information is unavailable and what efforts were made to obtain it. (Deyo v. Kilbourne (1978) 84 CA3d 771, 782). The responses at issue here fail to do this. Interrogatories 3, 6, 9, 12, 21, 46, and 47 ask Roberts to identify all writings that support specific contentions made in the Petition. The response to each of these Interrogatories is a list of types of writings, which does not describe or summarize the documents individually. This is an inadequate response. If an interrogatory seeks information contained in files and records, the responding party is under a duty to provide "complete and straightforward" answers. (CCP section 2030.220(a).) Roberts has not done that here. Roberts' argument that these Special Interrogatories will be answered via other concurrent discovery is unavailing. Although the court may establish the sequence and timing of discovery "for the convenience of parties and witnesses and in the interests of justice," except as otherwise provided, "the methods of discovery may be used in any sequence." (CCP section 2019.020.) [Part 1 of 2]
